coredata.dc:title: On-site chemosensing and quantification of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater using one-step synthesized fluorescent carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hong-Yi
Li, Dong
Guo, Yun
Yang, Yang
Wei, Weili
Xie, Bing
coredata.dc:description: Wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry contains Cr(VI) and severely threatens the environment in China. The on-site monitoring and quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in this wastewater is urgently needed to protect the environment and human health. To address this issue, amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were easily synthesized as fluorescence probes. CQD fluorescence was selectively and sensitively quenched by Cr(VI) due to the formation of ionic bonds between Cr(VI) anions and the amino groups on the CQD surface. On-site semiquantitative Cr(VI) chemosensing was realized with a portable UV lamp easily, rapidly, and cost-efficiently, providing on-stream visual proof for the dischargeability of the waste stream. The CQDs-based quantitative Cr(VI) determination method for the industrial wastewater was established with a linear range spanning 4 orders of magnitude and a limit of detection as low as 140 nmol/L, far exceeding the Cr(VI) concentration limit for the discharge of Cr(VI)-containing industrial wastewater in China. This quantitative chemosensing method is highly selective and sensitive in complex solutions, including the wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry. Therefore, this CQDs-based chemosensing method can be used not only for on-site chemosensing and quantitative Cr(VI) concentration determination in wastewater, but has considerable potential for monitoring hazardous materials in other industrial effluents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chromium(VI)
Wastewater treatment
Fluorescence probe
Carbon quantum dots
Chemosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Semiquantitative chemosensing and quantification of Cr(VI)
Chemosensing of Cr(VI) by the CQDs
Effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence properties of the CQDs
On-site chemosensing of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater
Standard curve and limit of detection
Quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of CQDs by one-step hydrothermal method.
HRTEM images of CQDs: (a) Particle size and morphology analysis (Inset: Particle size distribution); (b) magnification showing lattice structure and spacing.
XRD pattern (a) and FT-IR spectrum (b) of CQDs.
XPS pattern (a) and C 1s peak (b) of CQDs.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs (a) and fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (b).
Relationship between the integrated fluorescence intensity and UV absorbance of CQDs and quinine sulfate.
UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI), CQDs (a) and their mixtures (b).
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs with the addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI).
(a) Quenching effect of each metal ion on the fluorescence of CQDs; (b) Effect of masking agent NaF (300 μmol/L) on the fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Cr(VI) and Fe3+ (each 100 μmol/L).
(a) Fluorescence comparison between CQDs, CQDs with 9.6 μmol/L Cr(VI), and CQDs with wastewater, respectively; (b) Portable UV lamp required for on-site chemosensing.
(a)–(e) Fluorescence responses of CQDs at different concentrations to Cr(VI), and (f) comparison of their quenching ratios.
Types and contents of the carbon atoms in CQDs.
Chemical composition of the AVP wastewater.

head.ce:title: On-site chemosensing and quantification of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater using one-step synthesized fluorescent carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry contains Cr(VI) and severely threatens the environment in China. The on-site monitoring and quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in this wastewater is urgently needed to protect the environment and human health. To address this issue, amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were easily synthesized as fluorescence probes. CQD fluorescence was selectively and sensitively quenched by Cr(VI) due to the formation of ionic bonds between Cr(VI) anions and the amino groups on the CQD surface. On-site semiquantitative Cr(VI) chemosensing was realized with a portable UV lamp easily, rapidly, and cost-efficiently, providing on-stream visual proof for the dischargeability of the waste stream. The CQDs-based quantitative Cr(VI) determination method for the industrial wastewater was established with a linear range spanning 4 orders of magnitude and a limit of detection as low as 140 nmol/L, far exceeding the Cr(VI) concentration limit for the discharge of Cr(VI)-containing industrial wastewater in China. This quantitative chemosensing method is highly selective and sensitive in complex solutions, including the wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry. Therefore, this CQDs-based chemosensing method can be used not only for on-site chemosensing and quantitative Cr(VI) concentration determination in wastewater, but has considerable potential for monitoring hazardous materials in other industrial effluents.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is one of the essential trace elements in the human body and is an important blood glucose regulator; a deficiency in Cr will lead to heart disease [1]. Chromium species exhibit different toxicities depending on their valence state. Cr(VI) is highly toxic, with a toxicity nearly 100 times that of Cr(III). Additionally, Cr(VI) is more easily absorbed by the human body and can accumulate in bodily tissues [2], eventually leading to the occurrence of rhinitis, bronchitis, adenocarcinoma, lung cancer, and other diseases [3]. Therefore, the monitoring and control of Cr(VI) is of great significance for environmental protection and human health.

Cr(VI) is widely found in industrial wastewater streams. Upon effluent discharge, Cr(VI) enters into the soil and aquatic biome in the form of soluble anions (Cr2O7 2− or CrO4 2−), and is not easily digested by organisms [4]. Under the action of biological amplification, Cr(VI) is directly or indirectly absorbed by the human body through drinking water, food, the atmosphere, and other pathways, leading to cancerous lesions [5]. In China, the wastewater from industrial vanadium extraction is a typical Cr(VI)-containing effluent [6]. Worldwide, China is the leading producer and seller of vanadium products, supplying ∼90,000 tons V2O5 per year, principally extracted (∼90%) from vanadium-titanium magnetite ore [7]. Chromium is an important associated element in this ore, and undergoes the same chemical changes as V in the vanadium extraction process due to their highly similar physicochemical properties. Thus, Cr is present in the extracted solution in the form of Cr(VI) [8]. After the precipitation of V(V), Cr(VI) remains in the approximately 2,000,000 tons of wastewater produced per year on average. Although a reduction-precipitation method has been adopted to convert Cr(VI) to Cr(OH)3 (which is then sequestered), it is difficult to completely reduce Cr(VI) to reach the discharge standard of 0.5 mg/L for Cr(VI)-containing wastewater [9]. Therefore, the monitoring of Cr(VI) in the discharge streams from the vanadium extraction industry is very important. Furthermore, the on-site semiquantitative determination of Cr(VI) in the vanadium extraction waste stream is critical (although currently unavailable) toward evaluating its dischargeability. In addition, quantitative determination is needed to control the extent of reduction and corresponding reduction conditions for Cr(VI). However, at present, the Cr(VI) content in the wastewater can be only analyzed off-site in a laboratory, which is time-consuming and prevents real-time monitoring. Therefore, a Cr(VI) determination method is urgently demanded which (1) can realize rapid on-site semiquantitative determination; (2) is a simple and inexpensive quantitative method applicable industrially; and (3) is highly sensitive and selective, considering the complex composition (including a variety of other metal ions) and the high physicochemical similarity between Cr(VI) and V(V).

Nowadays, the common Cr(VI) determination methods are electrochemical analysis, high performance liquid chromatography, ion chromatography, atomic absorption spectrometry, and mass spectrometry [10–13]. Although these methods are sensitive, they are not suitable for industrial applications due to their complicated sample preparation processes, expensive equipment, and lack of timely and rapid detection. Compared with the methods mentioned above, the fluorescence probe method has the advantages of simple sample pretreatment, fast response, a wide linear dynamic range, lower interference, and high sensitivity [14–16], which can satisfy the requirements of industrial application.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as fluorescent probes, have been widely used in the chemosensing of various ions due to their high chemical stability, excellent water solubility, high resistance to photobleaching, and low toxicity [15,17]. Pooja et al. established a chemosensing system for arsenite based on synthesized thiol-functionalized CQDs [18]. Dong et al. used polyamine-functionalized CQDs to detect copper ions [19], and Liu et al. achieved the detection of Hg2+ with a Ag/CQDs composite [20]. Zhang et al. used the fluorescence-enhancing effect of Fe3+ on CQDs to quantitatively determine Fe3+ in aqueous solution [21]. Gao et al. used the fluorescence-enhancing effect of Ag+ on CQDs to detect Ag+ [22], while Xu et al. prepared carbon dots incorporating flavonoid moieties for the detection and removal of Pb2+ from water [23]. Besides these metal cations, CQDs have been widely utilized to determine Cr(VI) anions by fluorescence-quenching effect of Cr(VI) with LODs ranging from 0.28 μmol/L to 2.10 mol/L, which LODs are unsatisfactory [24–28]. Nevertheless, these reports indicate that CQDs are leading and promising fluorescent probes for the chemosensing of Cr(VI) ions.

In this paper, amino-functionalized CQDs with fluorescence quantum yields of up to 49.5% were synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The morphology, chemical composition, and surface functional groups of the CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The fluorescence properties of the CQDs were characterized by ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The quenching effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence of CQDs was investigated systematically. Based on these results, a method for both on-site semiquantitative chemosensing and the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in the industrial wastewater from vanadium extraction was established using CQDs as fluorescent probes, which offers high selectivity and sensitivity with LOD of 0.14 μmol/L, and is operationally rapid and simple. Furthermore, the synthesis of the CQD fluorescent probes was inexpensive and simple, since the synthesis and surface functionalization were completed in the same step. Therefore, the described Cr(VI) determination method should be suitable for application in the vanadium extraction industry, and will lay a technical foundation for the development of green vanadium production lines. Meanwhile, it also provides new insights for the on-site monitoring and quantification of Cr(VI) in other industrial wastewater streams.

Citric acid (4.204 g), ethylenediamine (1.34 mL), and deionized water (40 mL) were combined with stirring. The mixed solution was placed in a 50-mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was heated at 200 °C for 5 h; the product after the reaction was cooled to room temperature to obtain the initial CQD solution. This solution was dialyzed with a molecular weight cut-off of 3.5 kDa in the dark for 24 h to remove the residual small-molecule impurities. After 24 h of lyophilization, CQD powder was obtained.

The morphology and phase of the CQDs were characterized with a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, LIBRA 200 FEG, ZEISS, Germany) and an X-ray diffractometer (D/max 2500 v/pc, Rigaku, Japan; Cu Kα (λ = 0.15405 nm), scanning speed: 4°/min), respectively. A Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution, HORIBA, France), an FT-IR system (Nicolet 5DXC, Nicolet, USA), and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were used to characterize the chemical composition and functional groups of the CQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CQDs were collected with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, USA), respectively. Quinine sulfate with a fluorescence quantum yield of 54% was used as the standard to measure the quantum yield of the CQDs at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Semiquantitative chemosensing and quantification of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Cr(VI) solutions (0.50–19,200 μmol/L) were obtained by the sequential dilution of a Cr(VI) stock solution (10 mmol/L), and the pH was adjusted to 6–7 with NaOH (1 mol/L). A CQD stock solution (500 μg/mL) was also prepared. For semiquantitative chemosensing, the CQD stock solution was mixed with deionized H2O, diluted Cr(VI) solution, and industrial wastewater from vanadium extraction, respectively, to achieve a final CQD concentration of 1.0 μg/mL in each solution. In the reference solution, the final Cr(VI) concentration was 9.6 μmol/L; the dilution of industrial wastewater was negligible. Fluorescence intensities of the three solutions were observed comparatively with a portable UV lamp (WFH-204B, Keqin, China) at 365 nm. For quantitative determination, the Cr(VI) solutions and 1.0 μg/mL CQD solution were mixed in a volume ratio of 1:1, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of the mixed solution (I) and the 0.50 μg/mL CQD solution (I 0) were measured at λex = 340 nm. The Cr(VI) concentration in the wastewater was determined after a standard curve was constructed by plotting the quenching ratio I 0/I on the y-axis.

With citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the surface modifier, functionalized CQDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. As shown in Fig. 1 , the synthesis and surface functionalization of the CQDs are simultaneously completed in the hydrothermal process, omitting the extra step of passivation following the synthesis step. Usually, CQD surfaces contain very reactive functional groups such as COOH, which are disadvantageous to the binding of Cr(VI) ions. Thus, surface passivation and functionalization are needed to modify the CQD surface for the easier and stronger binding of Cr(VI). The simultaneous synthesis and amide surface functionalization of these CQDs makes their preparation facile, simple, and rapid, which is especially attractive for industrial analysis. The –NH2 functional groups provide actives sites for the formation of ionic bonds between the Cr(VI) anions and CQD surface in acidic media, enabling the chemosensing and determination of Cr(VI) by the CQDs.

The morphology of the CQDs was characterized by HRTEM. Fig. 2 (a) reveals that the CQDs are nearly spherical, with particle sizes distributed mainly between 2 and 4 nm and an average particle size of ∼3.2 nm. The CQDs also exhibit good dispersibility in aqueous solution. Fig. 2(b) shows that the lattice is regular and continuous, confirming the crystallinity of the CQDs. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the typical distance between graphite (100) planes [29], indicating that the crystal structure of the CQDs is mainly composed of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms [30] and the synthesized CQDs have a graphite-like structure.

The phase structure of the CQDs was characterized by XRD (Fig. 3 (a)). The XRD pattern shows a resolvable broad diffraction peak at 25.4°, which is close to the diffraction peak of the graphite (002) crystal plane at 26.1°. The sharp diffraction peak at 49.2° is close to the diffraction peak of graphite (102) plane at 50.1°. These findings suggest that the bulk crystallinity of the CQDs is incomplete [31]; the decreases in the diffraction angles imply increases in the CQD (002) plane distance and (102) plane distance, which are caused by the CO and CN bonds in the CQDs. Thus, the CQDs have a graphite-like structure with incomplete bulk crystallinity, which is consistent with the HRTEM characterization results. In addition, the weak sharp peak at 54.7° is indexed to the diffraction peak of graphite (004) crystal plane, which indicates the existence of minor well-grown graphite crystals. According to the diffraction peak at 25.4°, the particle size of the CQDs was calculated to be about 2.9 nm by the Scherrer equation, which agrees with the statistical particle size results in Fig. 2(a).

To understand the graphitization degree of the CQDs and the types of functional groups they contain, Raman spectroscopy and FT-IR analyses were carried out. As shown in Fig. S1, due to the influence of the intrinsic fluorescence of the CQDs [32], the G peak (indicative of crystallinity) at 1598 cm−1 and the D peak (indicative of the disorder degree) at 1350 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum are not significant and could not be used to quantitatively characterize the graphitization degree of the CQDs. The FT-IR spectrum is shown in Fig. 3(b). The CQDs display two sharp peaks at 2927 and 1402 cm−1, representing the stretching and bending vibration peaks of the CH bond, respectively, and indicate the presence of saturated hydrocarbon groups. The strong peak at 3440 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration peak of an NH bond in an amide group, while the two strong peaks at 1692 and 1652 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of an amido CO bond, indicating that the synthesized CQDs contain amide groups (−CO–NH–). The amide bonds were formed between the –NH2 groups of the surface modifier ethylenediamine and the COOH groups on the CQD surface, which demonstrates that the CQD surface was successfully passivated and functionalized by ethylenediamine in the simple one-step hydrothermal process. In addition, the strong peak at 1559 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane bending vibration of the NH bond in NH2 groups; the medium-intensity shoulder peak at ∼3200 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the NH bond in NH2 groups, and this vibration also enhances the intensity of the peak at 3440 cm−1. Thus, free NH2 groups, the unbonded termini of the ethylenediamine units, exist on the CQD surface, and are expected to form ionic bonds between Cr(VI) anions in acidic solution.

To explore the optical properties of the synthesized CQDs, the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission properties of the CQDs were systematically investigated. Fig. 5 (a) shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs. Absorption mainly occurs in the ultraviolet region. A strong characteristic absorption maximum is observed at 340 nm, due to the n–π* transition of the π-electrons and the π–π* transition of electrons in the conjugated CC double bonds in the CQDs [33].

Fig. 5(b) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized CQDs at excitation wavelengths of 300–440 nm. When λex = 340 nm, the CQDs exhibit the highest fluorescence emission intensity, affording a blue-purple emission spectrum with a maximum emission wavelength (λem) of 440 nm. The λex is consistent with the maximum absorption wavelength of 340 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Notably, the synthesized CQDs also exhibit high fluorescence intensity at λex = 360 nm. At λex = 300–380 nm, with increasing excitation wavelength, the redshift of the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CQDs is small and only the fluorescence intensity changes. When λex > 380 nm, the CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence: the maximum λem is redshifted as the λex increases. When the λex is increased to 440 nm, the maximum λem of the CQDs is significantly redshifted ( + 40 nm). Due to the excitation-dependence of the CQD fluorescence, λex was set at 340 nm in the subsequent study of the effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence properties of the synthesized CQDs.

To further evaluate the fluorescence performance of the synthesized CQDs, a key indicator of fluorescence, the quantum yield, was adopted. Quinine sulfate, with a quantum yield of 54%, was used as the reference for measuring the quantum yield of the synthesized CQDs due to its similar excitation wavelength. The UV–vis absorption spectrum and the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs and quinine sulfate were analyzed, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . The calculated quantum yield of the CQDs is 49.5%, which is significantly higher than those of common CQDs (<10%) [34]. Although CQDs with a high quantum yield were now less rare, including those with quantum yield of 20.1%–39.7% [24,35–38] and 55.4% [26], most of them are still surpassed by the CQDs prepared in this work. Thus, the CQDs synthesized by our facile one-step hydrothermal method could function as a highly sensitive fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemosensing of Cr(VI) by the CQDs
Effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence properties of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of Cr(VI) on the fluorescence properties of the CQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of a Cr(VI) solution, the CQDs, and their mixed solutions were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the Cr(VI) solution evidences obvious characteristic absorption peaks at 270 and 365 nm, while the CQDs afford an obvious characteristic absorption peak at 340 nm; both spectra overlap in the wavelength region around 350 nm. Fig. 7(b) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of mixed solutions containing 0.1 to 100 μmol/L Cr(VI), and the inset shows the magnification of the characteristic peaks. The two characteristic absorption peaks of Cr(VI) are not observed, whereas that due to the CQDs is evident at 340 nm. Meanwhile, as the Cr(VI) concentration increases from 0.1 to 100 μmol/L, the intensity of the CQDs characteristic absorption peak increases and is gradually redshifted (a redshift of 3.7 nm). This indicates that Cr(VI) anions have formed ionic bonds with the –NH2 groups on the CQD surface, which extends the p-π conjugated system, resulting in the redshift.

Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs mixed with different concentrations of Cr(VI). As the Cr(VI) concentration increases from 0.1 to 100 μmol/L, the CQDs fluorescence intensity at 440 nm decreases gradually, which indicates that Cr(VI) has an obvious fluorescence-quenching effect on the CQDs. In combination with the above observed absorption intensification in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs mixed with Cr(VI) solutions, it can be deduced that Cr(VI) anions quench the fluorescence of the synthesized CQDs via bonding with them. The chemical bonding responsible for the fluorescence quenching of CQDs was verified by the fluorescence spectra in absence and presence of Cr(VI) ligands of Diphenylcarbazide (Fig. S2). XPS patterns of CQDs showed that the N 1s peak shifted positively and its intensity decreased after mixing with Cr(VI), further indicating hydrogen bond formation between N atoms on CQD surface and O atoms in Cr(VI) anions (Fig. S3). Based on the fluorescence-quenching effect of Cr(VI) on the CQDs, the CQDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for chemosensing and quantitative determination of Cr(VI) anions. The pH of Cr(VI) solution was controlled at 6–7 since targeting at application in AVP wastewater, although fluorescence quenching ratio of Cr(VI) is most sensitive at pH 1.0 (Fig. S4).

The selectivity and anti-interference performance of the synthesized CQDs in the chemosensing of Cr(VI) were investigated. Since the vanadium extraction wastewater may contain V(V) anions, Ca2+, Al3+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe3+ and other metal ions in addition to Cr(VI) anions, the effects of these ions on the fluorescence properties of the CQDs were studied. In particular, due to the highly similar chemical properties of V(V) and Cr(VI) anions [8], the quenching effect of 50 μmol/L Cr(VI) in the presence of 100 μmol/L V(V) on the fluorescence of the CQDs was investigated (Fig. S5). The fluorescence-quenching ratio is defined as the original fluorescence intensity of the CQD solution (I 0) divided by the fluorescence intensity of the metal ion-spiked CQD solution (I), denoted as I 0/I. The fluorescence-quenching ratio of Cr(VI) on the CQDs is not influenced, even in the presence of V(V) at a concentration twice that of Cr(VI). This indicates that the CQDs have high selectivity for the chemosensing and detection of Cr(VI). The quenching ratios of each metal ion (mentioned above) at 100 μmol/L on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs are compared in Fig. 9 (a). Except for the slight quenching effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of the CQDs (I 0/I = 1.53), other metal ions do not enhance or quench the fluorescence (I 0/I values fluctuating around 1), showing the anti-interference performance of the CQDs in the chemosensing and detection of Cr(VI). To exclude the interference of Fe3+, NaF was used as a masking agent to mask Fe3+. Fig. 9(b) shows the quenching effects of the Cr(VI) and Fe3+ solutions with and without NaF on the fluorescence of the CQDs. Clearly, the addition of NaF does not significantly influence the quenching ratio of Cr(VI) on CQD fluorescence. In comparison, after NaF is added to the Fe3+ solution as a masking agent, fluorescence quenching of the CQDs no longer occurs, and the interference of Fe3+ on the quenching of CQD fluorescence by Cr(VI) is thus effectively eliminated. These results demonstrate that the use of NaF to mask potentially interfering Fe3+ in the analyte solution can further ensure the high selectivity and anti-interference performance of the CQDs in the sensing and detection of Cr(VI), which guarantees that the synthesized CQDs are suitable for the chemosensing of Cr(VI) in complex solutions, including the wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site chemosensing of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The industrial wastewater from vanadium extraction was collected at the production line and the on-site chemosensing of Cr(VI) was conducted. Considering that most commercial portable UV lamps emit UV light at wavelengths of 254 and 365 nm, the fluorescence of the CQDs was excited at 365 nm when conducting the on-site chemosensing of Cr(VI), since the synthesized CQDs were shown to exhibit high fluorescence intensity at λex = 360 nm (Fig. 5(b)). To quickly evaluate the dischargeability of wastewater in real time, semiquantitative chemosensing was performed using 9.6 μmol/L (0.5 mg/L) Cr(VI) solution as the reference. This concentration is the standard for the highest allowable Cr(VI) concentration in the discharge of Cr(VI)-containing industrial wastewater in China [9]. CQD solutions (2 μg/mL) were mixed with 9.6 μmol/L Cr(VI) (final concentrations) and wastewater, respectively. Due to the high concentration of the CQDs stock solution used during mixing, any dilution of the wastewater can be ignored. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), the CQDs strongly fluoresce at λex = 365 nm, which is quenched partially in the presence of 9.6 μmol/L Cr(VI) anions; in comparison, the CQD fluorescence is visibly and completely quenched by the wastewater, which indicates that the Cr(VI) concentration in the wastewater is higher than 9.6 μmol/L. Thus, this wastewater cannot be discharged directly into the environment. This visualized comparison between the wastewater and reference solution (9.6 μmol/L Cr(VI)) quickly provides semiquantitative proof for judging the dischargeability of the wastewater. In addition, the only equipment required is the portable UV lamp shown in Fig. 10(b), which enables the on-site monitoring of Cr(VI) concentrations at the industrial production line. Therefore, the CQD-based on-site semiquantitative Cr(VI) chemosensing method is practical for monitoring the wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry, and can provide new insights for the on-site monitoring of hazardous materials in other industrial waste streams.

To improve sensitivity in the quantitative determination of Cr(VI), the concentration of the synthesized CQDs as a fluorescent probe was optimized. The fluorescence spectra of the CQD solutions at 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 μg/mL before and after combining with 100 μmol/L Cr(VI) are shown in Fig. 11 (a)–(e), respectively. The fluorescence-quenching ratios at all CQD concentrations are compared in Fig. 11(f). When the CQD concentration is 0.5 μg/mL, the highest fluorescence-quenching ratio of 2.330 is achieved, indicating that the CQD solution at this concentration is the most sensitive in the chemosensing of Cr(VI). Therefore, 0.5 μg/mL CQD solution was used for the subsequent quantitative determinations of Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curve and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The extent of the CQDs fluorescence quenching differs predictably when the Cr(VI) concentration is varied. When the CQD concentration is 0.5 μg/mL, the CQDs fluorescence intensity decreases gradually after being mixed with 0.25–25 μmol/L Cr(VI). The fluorescence-quenching ratios of the CQDs by Cr(VI) at different concentrations were calculated, and the results were subjected to linear regression analysis. The obtained equation,
y = 1.080 + 0.015 x
(R 2 = 0.997), reveals good linearity. This standard curve is thus applicable in the determination of Cr(VI) in concentration range of 0.25–25 μmol/L.

To obtain the lower LOD of this method, 11 blank samples were determined in parallel and the results are shown in Table S1. The relative standard deviation among the samples is 0.068%. The LOD, obtained by dividing the 3-fold standard deviation by the slope of the standard curve (above), is calculated to be 0.14 μmol/L (7.28 μg/L). Compared with other fluorescence methods for Cr(VI) detection, this method has greatly improved detection sensitivity [39–41]. Even compared with other reported CQDs with LOD of 0.28 μmol/L [24], 0.4 μmol/L [28], 0.52 μmol/L [25], 1.17 μmol/L [27] and 2.10 mol/L [26], the CQDs synthesized in this work exhibit obviously lower LOD which indicates higher sensitivity. In addition, The LOD value is considerably lower than the highest limit (0.5 mg/L) for Cr(VI) concentration for the discharge of Cr(VI)-containing industrial wastewater in China [9], showing the potential of this CQDs-based method in the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in industrial waste streams.

The relationship between the Cr(VI) concentration and corresponding fluorescence-quenching ratio of the CQDs was further studied. When the CQD concentration was 0.5 μg/mL, Cr(VI) was added to achieve final concentrations of 19.2–9600 μmol/L (1–500 μg/mL, respectively). The fluorescence intensities were determined, and the corresponding fluorescence-quenching ratios were calculated. The fitting results show a good linear relationship between the fluorescence-quenching ratio and Cr(VI) concentration, affording the linear equation
y = 2.032 + 0.071 x
(R 2 = 0.998). These results reveal that the CQDs are also applicable in the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) at high concentrations, indicating that the linear range for Cr(VI) detection by the synthesized CQDs fluorescence probes is wide and spans 4 orders of magnitude.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of Cr(VI) anions in the industrial wastewater from vanadium extraction was thus determined by the CQD-based method, and the chemical composition of the wastewater determined by inductively coupled plasma–atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) is shown in Table 2 . The industrial wastewater was diluted by 50 times and the CQD concentration was maintained at 0.5 μg/mL after mixing. The fluorescence-quenching ratio was determined to be 1.33 and the Cr(VI) concentration was calculated to be 16.91 μmol/L according to the standard curve. These results are consistent with the concentration of 16.93 μmol/L determined by ICP-AES, with a relative standard deviation of 0.12%. Thus, the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) based on the synthesized CQDs as fluorescence probes is effective and reliable.

To verify that this quantitative method is suitable for application in the industrial wastewater from vanadium extraction, spike-and-recovery experiments were performed on the wastewater. After the wastewater samples were diluted by 50, 100, and 400 times, respectively, 7.00, 5.00, and 1.00 μmol/L Cr(VI) solutions were added, respectively. Parallel measurements were performed in triplicate, and the recoveries were calculated. As shown in Table 3 , the recoveries range from 98.00% to 107.00%. These results indicate that the established method based on CQDs as fluorescence probes is suitable for the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) in the wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry.

In this paper, graphite-like CQDs were synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. The CQD synthesis and surface amino functionalization were realized simultaneously, making preparation simple and inexpensive. The synthesized CQDs emitted fluorescence at 440 nm for λex = 340 nm, with a high quantum yield of up to 49.5%. The fluorescence of the synthesized CQDs could be selectively and sensitively quenched by Cr(VI) due to the formation of ionic bonds between the Cr(VI) anions and amino groups on the CQD surface. Using these CQDs, the on-site semiquantitative chemosensing of Cr(VI) in wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry was realized with a portable UV lamp, which quickly and cost-efficiently provides visual proof for the dischargeability of wastewater at the production line with easy operability. The CQD-based quantitative determination of Cr(VI) has also been established with a linear range spanning 4 orders of magnitude and an LOD as low as 0.14 μmol/L (7.28 μg/L), which is much lower than the limit for Cr(VI) concentration in the discharge standard of Cr(VI)-containing industrial wastewater in China. For Cr(VI) concentrations in the 0.25–25 μmol/L range, the standard curve was
y = 1.080 + 0.015 x
(R 2 = 0.997); whereas, for 19.2–9600 μmol/L Cr(VI), the standard curve was
y = 2.032 + 0.071 x
(R 2 = 0.998). This quantitative method is applicable in the sensitive determination of the Cr(VI) concentration in aqueous solution, including complex systems such as wastewater from the vanadium extraction industry. Therefore, this method based on easily synthesized CQDs allows not only on-site chemosensing but also the quantitative determination of Cr(VI) concentrations in industrial wastewater with high selectivity and sensitivity, which provides new insights for monitoring hazardous materials in other industrial waste streams.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51474041, 51674051), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (106112015CDJZR465505, cqu2017hbrc1B08).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.157.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Prof. Hong-Yi Li obtained her PhD in Analytical Chemistry in 2010 in Peking University, China. She was a joint PhD student during 2008–2009 in École Supérieure de Physique et de Chimie Industrielles, Université de Pierre et Marie Curie, France. She began to work as a lecturer in College of Materials Sciences and Engineering, Chongqing University in 2010. In 2011, she became an associate professor and was promoted to be a professor in 2015. Her research interests focus on the separation and analysis of heavy metal ions for the metal extraction purpose.
